description: Common preservative; may form benzene with vitamin C and is linked to hyperactivity in children.
Raises LDL cholesterol and lowers HDL; a major cardiovascular risk and banned in many countries.
Used in cured meats; associated with formation of nitrosamines and colorectal cancer risk.
Curing agent that converts to nitrite; linked to increased cancer risk with high intake.
Cheap sweetener strongly linked to obesity, fatty liver and metabolic syndrome.
Dough strengthener banned in the EU, UK and many countries as a possible carcinogen.
Dough conditioner banned in the EU/Australia; degradation products are of safety concern.
Emulsifier in some sodas; bromine can accumulate in tissue. Banned in several countries.
Artificial sweetener; may trigger headaches in sensitive people and unsafe for those with PKU.
Flavour enhancer that can cause transient reactions (headache, flushing) in sensitive individuals.
Synthetic yellow dye linked to allergic reactions and hyperactivity in some children.
Synthetic dye associated with hyperactivity and allergic responses in sensitive children.
Synthetic red dye linked to hyperactivity in children; restricted in some regions.
Preservative classified as a possible human carcinogen (IARC Group 2B).
Preservative with possible endocrine-disrupting effects in animal studies.
Seaweed-derived thickener that may promote gut inflammation in some people.
Preservative that can trigger asthma and breathing problems in sensitive individuals.
Sulphite preservative; a known trigger for asthma and allergic reactions.
Antioxidant with some animal evidence of endocrine and reproductive effects.
High in saturated fat; frequent intake can raise cholesterol. Also an environmental concern.
Older artificial sweetener once linked to cancer; now generally regarded as safe in normal amounts.
Artificial sweetener considered safe by regulators but with limited long-term data.
Widely used colouring; some forms (E150d) contain trace 4-MEI, a compound under study.
Highly processed carbohydrate with a high glycaemic index; spikes blood sugar quickly.
Generic synthetic colouring; several specific dyes are linked to hyperactivity in children.
Generic synthetic flavouring; usually safe but masks the absence of real ingredients.
Essential base ingredient with no health concern.
Common grain base; choose wholegrain where possible.
Natural dairy source of protein and calcium.
High-quality dairy protein commonly used in foods and supplements.
Wholegrain rich in soluble fibre that supports heart health.
Essential vitamin and natural antioxidant; beneficial.
Fat-soluble vitamin used as a natural antioxidant; beneficial.
Naturally occurring acid used safely as a flavour and preservative.
Natural emulsifier from soy or sunflower; well tolerated.
Natural fruit-derived gelling agent; also a source of fibre.
Common leavening agent considered safe in food amounts.
Natural cocoa rich in flavonoid antioxidants.
Natural sweetener; still high in sugar, so use in moderation.
Heart-healthy unsaturated fat rich in antioxidants.
Natural leavening agent used in baking; safe.
Protein-based gelling agent; safe but not vegetarian.
Plant-based fibre thickener; safe and adds soluble fibre.
Fermentation-derived thickener regarded as safe.
Mineral used for fortification and as an anti-caking agent; safe.
Essential but easy to over-consume; high intake raises blood pressure.
Natural sweetener; excess intake is linked to weight gain and tooth decay.
